topic: Maratha Empire — Shivaji, Peshwas & Panipat III

questions.text: Shivaji was crowned as 'Chhatrapati' at which fort?
questions.explanation: Shivaji was crowned Chhatrapati (Supreme Sovereign) on June 6, 1674 CE at Raigad Fort (Maharashtra). The ceremony was performed by Gagabhatta (a Brahmin from Varanasi). Raigad Fort is also where Shivaji died in 1680 CE and is now a major historic monument.

questions.text: The Third Battle of Panipat (1761 CE) was fought between the Marathas and:
questions.explanation: The Third Battle of Panipat (January 14, 1761) was between the Maratha Empire (under Sadashivrao Bhau and Viswas Rao) and Ahmad Shah Abdali (Durrani, Afghan king) allied with the Rohillas and Nawab Shuja-ud-Daula of Awadh. The Marathas suffered a catastrophic defeat.

questions.text: Who was Shivaji's mother, considered the most important influence on his life?
questions.explanation: Jijabai (Jijamata) was Shivaji's mother — the most profound influence on his character, instilling pride in Hindu traditions, the concept of Hindavi Swarajya, and determination to build an independent kingdom. She died just 12 days after Shivaji's coronation in 1674. She is revered as a saint in Maharashtra.

questions.text: The Peshwa was the title given to which office in the Maratha administration?
questions.explanation: Peshwa = prime minister in Shivaji's Ashtapradhan (council of eight ministers). After Shivaji's death, the Peshwas of the Bhat family (Pune) gradually became the de facto rulers of the Maratha Empire, while the Chhatrapati remained nominal. The Peshwaship lasted until the Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817–18).

questions.text: Shivaji used which concealed weapon against Afzal Khan at the Battle of Pratapgarh (1659)?
questions.explanation: Shivaji used Bagh Nakh ('tiger claws') — a concealed iron weapon worn across the knuckles with five curved blades — when he embraced Afzal Khan during their supposed peace meeting at Pratapgarh. He drove these into the Khan's belly, mortally wounding him. The Bagh Nakh is now preserved at the Satara Museum and the Victoria and Albert Museum (London).

questions.text: What were 'Chauth' and 'Sardeshmukhi' — the two Maratha taxes?
options: Chauth = 1/10th of revenue; Sardeshmukhi = 1/4th of revenue
Chauth = 1/4th of revenue as protection tax; Sardeshmukhi = additional 10%
Chauth = tribute from Hindu kingdoms; Sardeshmukhi = tribute from Muslim kingdoms
Chauth = tax on merchants; Sardeshmukhi = tax on landlords
questions.explanation: Chauth = 1/4th (one-quarter) of the land revenue of a territory — paid to the Marathas as 'protection.' Sardeshmukhi = an additional 10% of land revenue — claimed as the hereditary right of the Maratha Chhatrapati as 'chief headman' of the Deccan. Together, these gave the Marathas enormous economic power over territories they didn't directly rule.

questions.text: Baji Rao I, the greatest Peshwa, is described as having 'never lost a battle.' In his famous march to Delhi (~1737), he demonstrated:
options: Lightning cavalry tactics covering 900+ km to Delhi's outskirts
questions.explanation: Baji Rao I's 1737 march to Delhi was a lightning cavalry raid — covering over 900 km from the Deccan to the outskirts of Delhi in a matter of weeks. The Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah was panic-stricken. The raid demonstrated the Marathas' ability to project power across all of India. Baji Rao argued: 'Strike at the trunk; the branches will fall.'

questions.text: Shivaji escaped from Aurangzeb's custody in Agra by hiding in:
options: A hay cart leaving the fort
Large baskets (of sweets/fruit) carried out of his house arrest
A river boat on the Yamuna
Disguised as a Sufi saint with followers
questions.explanation: Shivaji escaped from house arrest in Agra in 1666 CE — the most popular version says he hid in large baskets (of sweets, fruits, or goods) that were routinely sent out as charity. His son Sambhaji was smuggled out separately. They made their way through Rajasthan and back to Maharashtra in disguise.

questions.text: What was Shivaji's administrative council called, and how many ministers did it have?
questions.explanation: Shivaji's council was called the Ashtapradhan (Council of Eight Ministers): Peshwa (PM), Mazumdar (accountant), Dabir (foreign secretary), Senapati/Sar-i-Naubat (military chief), Nyayadhish (chief justice), Panditrao (religious affairs), Sumant (superintendent of state/household), and Waqianavish (correspondence). The Peshwa was first among equals.

questions.text: Sambhaji (Shivaji's son) was captured by Aurangzeb and executed in which year?
questions.explanation: Sambhaji was captured in February 1689 CE at Sangameshwar (Maharashtra) and was publicly tortured and executed (March 1689) by Aurangzeb — reportedly for refusing to convert to Islam. His martyrdom galvanised Maratha resistance rather than ending it. His brother Rajaram continued the fight.

questions.text: Tarabai (regent 1700–1707) who effectively led Maratha resistance after Rajaram's death was:
options: Rajaram's widow and mother of Shivaji II
questions.explanation: Tarabai was Rajaram's widow (Shivaji's daughter-in-law). After Rajaram died (1700), she acted as regent for her son Shivaji II and effectively commanded the Maratha armies. She raided deep into Mughal territory — into Malwa and Gujarat — while Aurangzeb was still alive and supposedly besieging Maratha forts. She is one of India's greatest female military leaders.

questions.text: The Treaty of Salbai (1782), which ended the First Anglo-Maratha War, resulted in:
options: The Peshwa becoming a British pensioner
Status quo — roughly the pre-war boundaries restored
The Marathas receiving Surat from the British
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Salbai (May 1782) ended the First Anglo-Maratha War essentially as a status quo agreement — each side returned most captured territories. The British failed to impose their preferred candidate on the Peshwaship. This was actually a setback for British ambitions in the Deccan — they had to wait for the Second and Third Anglo-Maratha Wars to gain supremacy.

questions.text: The greatest Peshwa who 'never lost a battle' was:
questions.explanation: Baji Rao I (1720–1740 CE) is described as never having lost a battle. He expanded the Maratha Empire into Malwa, Gujarat, Bundelkhand, and raided Delhi in 1737. He died young at 39. He is regarded as one of the greatest cavalry commanders in Indian history. Madhav Rao I is second — he rebuilt the empire after Panipat III.

questions.text: The Three Anglo-Maratha Wars ultimately ended Maratha power. The Peshwaship was abolished after which war?
questions.explanation: The Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817–1818) resulted in the defeat of Peshwa Bajirao II and the formal abolition of the Peshwaship. Bajirao II was pensioned off at Bithur (near Kanpur). His adopted son Nana Saheb (Dhondu Pant) later led the 1857 revolt. British supremacy over all of India was established after 1818.

questions.text: The concept of 'Hindavi Swarajya' (self-rule of the Hindus) was most closely associated with:
questions.explanation: Hindavi Swarajya — the concept of self-rule (Swaraj) for the people of the land (Hindavi) — is most closely associated with Shivaji Maharaj. The term appears in his own correspondence. It was not narrowly religious but meant freedom from Mughal/foreign dominance for the people of the Deccan. Shivaji's mother Jijabai instilled this vision in him.

questions.text: Who is traditionally called the 'Father of the Indian Navy' for building a naval fleet to protect the Konkan coast?
questions.explanation: Shivaji is traditionally called the 'Father of the Indian Navy' for recognising the importance of sea power and building a significant Maratha naval fleet for the Konkan coast. He built sea forts like Sindhudurg (1664) and Vijaydurg. Kanhoji Angre was a later Maratha admiral who successfully fought the British and Portuguese at sea.
